assets.title_en: Blades and Mountings for a Pair of Swords (Daishō) — Paired Swords with Lacquer Mountings
assets.period: sword (katana) blade, dated 1440; short sword (wakizashi) blade, 15th century; mountings, late 18th century
assets.medium: Steel, wood, lacquer, copper-silver alloy (shibuichi), gold, copper, rayskin, silk
assets.dimensions: L. of sword (katana) 34 1/8 in. (86.7 cm); L. of sword (katana) blade 25 1/4 in. (64.1 cm); L. of sword (katana) scabbard 28 1/4 in. (71.8 cm); L. of short sword (wakizashi) 26 in. (66.0 cm); L. of short sword (wakizashi) blade 20 3/4 in. (52.7 cm); L. of short sword (wakizashi) scabbard 19 3/4 in. (50.2 cm); L. of knife (kozuka) for short sword (wakizashi) mounting 8 3/4 in. (22.2 cm)
assets.description: A magnificent paired set of long and short swords — the katana and wakizashi that together form a daishō, the defining badge of the samurai class. The blades date to the golden age of Japanese swordmaking during the Muromachi period, while the elaborate lacquer mountings were crafted centuries later during the Edo period, reflecting the enduring reverence for fine blades.
assets.significance: The daishō symbolized the samurai's dual nature: the long sword (katana) for combat, the short sword (wakizashi) for close quarters and ritual. Wearing both was a privilege restricted to the samurai class by Tokugawa law.
assets.image_alt: Blades and Mountings for a Pair of Swords (Daishō), 1440/15th century/late 18th century, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: Blade for a Sword (Katana) — Signed and Dated 1526
assets.title_jp: 刀 銘入 享禄二年
assets.medium: Steel
assets.dimensions: L. 36 1/8 in. (91.8 cm); L. of blade edge 29 9/16 in. (75.1 cm); D. of curvature 3/32 in. (2.4 cm)
assets.description: A signed and dated katana blade from the turbulent late Muromachi period — an era of constant warfare that demanded functional excellence. The blade's inscription places it precisely in 1526, making it a rare example of precisely dateable kotō craftsmanship. The graceful curvature and robust construction speak to a blade designed for real combat.
assets.significance: Signed and dated blades are exceptionally valuable for scholarship and authentication. This blade provides a fixed reference point for understanding regional forging techniques of the early 16th century.
assets.image_alt: Katana blade dated 1526, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.medium: Iron, lacquer, copper-gold alloy (shakudō), silver, silk, horse hair, ivory
assets.description: A complete suit of Edo-period samurai armor in remarkable condition. By the 18th century, armor had become largely ceremonial — a symbol of lineage and martial spirit rather than battlefield necessity. Yet the craftsmanship remains extraordinary: each plate lacquered, each cord precisely tied in the traditional patterns passed down through schools of armoring.
assets.significance: Edo-period gusoku represent the culmination of Japanese armoring technique, combining centuries of martial engineering with the decorative arts sensibility of a peaceful era.
assets.image_alt: Complete samurai armor (gusoku), The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: Blade for a Dagger (Tantō) — Kamakura Period
assets.medium: Steel
assets.dimensions: L. 13 5/8 in. (34.6 cm); L. of cutting edge 9 3/8 in. (23.8 cm); W. of blade at hilt 15/16 in. (2.4 cm); Wt. 7 oz. (185 g)
assets.description: A tantō (dagger) blade from the height of the Kamakura period — widely considered the golden age of Japanese swordmaking. The Mongol invasions of 1274 and 1281 had driven smiths to develop stronger, more resilient blades. This tantō exemplifies the Kamakura aesthetic: broad, sturdy, and purposeful, with a subtle hamon (temper line) that reveals the smith's mastery of differential hardening.
assets.significance: Kamakura-period blades are among the most prized in all of Japanese sword collecting. The tantō form was essential for close combat and ritual, and this example's 700-year-old provenance places it among the finest surviving examples.
assets.image_alt: Tantō blade from Kamakura period, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: 虫尽金具大小拵 Blade and Mounting for a Sword (Katana)
assets.medium: Steel, wood, lacquer, copper-gold alloy (shakudō), gold, copper-silver alloy (shibuichi), ray skin (same), silk
assets.dimensions: L. 37 7/8 in. (96.2 cm); L. of blade 35 9/16 in. (90.3 cm); L. of cutting edge 27 3/8 in. (69.6 cm); D. of curvatuer 3/4 in. (1.9 cm); L. of scabbard 29 in. (73.7 cm); L. of knife (kozuka) 8 1/2 in. (21.6 cm); L. of hair dressing tool (kogai) 8 1/4 in. (21.0 cm)
assets.description: An extraordinary katana where the beauty of the mountings rivals the blade itself. The fittings are decorated with an elaborate insect motif in shakudō (a blue-black copper-gold alloy unique to Japan), with cicadas, butterflies, and dragonflies rendered in exquisite detail. The blade, from the shintō (new sword) era, combines Edo-period refinement with martial function.
assets.significance: The 'insect gathering' motif demonstrates the Edo-period practice of elevating sword mountings to high art. These fittings were created by specialized metalworkers (kinko) whose skills were distinct from the swordsmiths who forged the blades.
assets.image_alt: Blade and Mounting for a Sword (Katana), 17th century blade/early 19th century mounting, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: Clog-shaped tea bowl (kutsu-gata chawan) with plum blossoms and geometric patterns — Clog-shaped Tea Bowl
assets.medium: Stoneware with iron-oxide decoration (Mino ware, Black Oribe type)
assets.dimensions: H. 3 in. (7.6 cm); W. 5 5/8 in. (14.3 cm); Diam. of foot 12 1/4 in. (5.7 cm)
assets.description: The deliberate warping of this Oribe-style tea bowl into the shape of a wooden clog (kutsu) transforms a functional vessel into a philosophical statement. Fired in the Mino kilns during the turbulent Momoyama-to-Edo transition, it carries the bold iron-oxide brushwork and vivid green glaze patches that distinguish Black Oribe ware. The plum blossom decoration rendered in loose, almost calligraphic strokes across the irregular surface reveals a potter who understood that beauty could emerge from controlled asymmetry.
assets.significance: Kutsu-gata chawan embody the radical aesthetic shift championed by tea master Furuta Oribe (1544–1615): the purposeful distortion of form as a rejection of perfection. Where Chinese ceramics pursued symmetrical perfection over centuries, Oribe potters bent still-soft vessels by hand, asserting that irregularity itself possessed deeper beauty. This philosophical reversal — that imperfection surpasses perfection — remains one of Japan's most enduring contributions to global aesthetics.
assets.image_alt: Clog-shaped tea bowl with plum blossoms, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.medium: Stoneware with white glassy glaze (Raku ware); gold lacquer repairs
assets.description: A red Raku tea bowl continuing the tradition established by Chōjirō under Sen no Rikyū's guidance in the 16th century. Raku bowls are hand-shaped (never thrown on a wheel), fired individually at low temperatures, and pulled from the kiln while still glowing — a process that yields each bowl's unique character. The warm red tone and soft, tactile surface invite the hands as much as the eyes.
assets.significance: The Raku family has maintained an unbroken lineage of tea bowl production for over 450 years, now in its 16th generation. Each generation's bowls reflect their individual artistic vision while honoring the founding principles of simplicity and directness.
assets.image_alt: Red Raku tea bowl, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: Shino Tea Bowl with Bridge and Shrine, Known as “Bridge of the Gods” (Shinkyō)
assets.medium: Stoneware with iron oxide under glaze (Mino ware, Shino type)
assets.description: A Shino-ware tea bowl bearing the poetic name 'Bridge of the Gods' (Kaminohashi), a reference to the sacred bridge at Nikkō's Tōshōgū shrine. The thick, milky-white Shino glaze — achieved through a feldspathic glaze over iron-oxide brushwork — creates a warm, inviting surface. The bridge motif, rendered in bold, spontaneous strokes, exemplifies the Momoyama aesthetic of vigor and directness.
assets.significance: Named tea bowls (meibutsu) occupy the highest echelon of Japanese ceramic culture. The act of naming bestows individual identity, transforming a functional vessel into a unique cultural artifact with its own biography.
assets.image_alt: Shino tea bowl 'Bridge of the Gods', The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: Under the Wave off Kanagawa (Kanagawa oki nami ura), or The Great Wave, from the series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji (Fugaku sanjūrokkei)
assets.medium: Woodblock print; ink and color on paper
assets.description: Three slender fishing vessels pitch helplessly as a colossal breaker curls overhead, its crest dissolving into clawed fingers of spray. Far beyond, snow-capped Fuji anchors the horizon in perfect stillness — an interplay of chaos and calm that has captivated audiences for nearly two centuries. Katsushika Hokusai designed this composition around 1831 at the height of his late-career mastery, exploiting the recently imported Prussian blue pigment to achieve the vivid, graduated tones that distinguish the finest impressions. The sheet belongs to a celebrated set depicting the sacred mountain from varied vantage points and seasons.
assets.significance: Arriving in European ports during the 1850s and 1860s, this single image reshaped Western visual culture more profoundly than any other work of Asian art. Debussy referenced its dynamism; Rilke meditated on its tension between fragility and force. At auction, exceptional early impressions now command seven-figure sums — the highest verified result reached $2.76 million at Christie's in 2023 — establishing this composition as the benchmark against which all Japanese print values are measured.
assets.image_alt: The Great Wave off Kanagawa by Katsushika Hokusai, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.medium: Woodblock print; ink on paper
assets.description: An early depiction of life within the Yoshiwara pleasure quarter — the licensed entertainment district that was the primary subject of ukiyo-e art. This painting predates the mass-produced woodblock prints that would later define the genre, offering a rare glimpse into the formative years of 'floating world' imagery. The scene captures courtesans, attendants, and patrons in an atmosphere of refined leisure.
assets.significance: Works from this early period document the transition from hand-painted ukiyo-e to the woodblock print medium. They reveal how the 'floating world' aesthetic — celebrating urban pleasure and beauty — emerged as a distinct artistic movement in the decades following the establishment of the Tokugawa shogunate.
assets.image_alt: Interior Scene in the Yoshiwara, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: Case (Inrō) with Design of Bean Vine
assets.medium: Case: powdered gold (maki-e) on lacquer with ceramic, lead, and mother-of-pearl inlays; ceramic seal on base; Fastener (ojime): alloy of silver and copper with design of mice in rice cakes; Toggle (netsuke): lacquer with design of lotus leaf and caterpillar in lead and ivory inlays
assets.image_alt: Case (Inrō) with Design of Bean Vine
assets.description: Five compartments nested together with cord channels and a sliding bead form this portable medicine case — an inrō that served as both personal accessory and status symbol for Edo-period men of rank. The surface depicts a bean vine meandering across the lacquered body, executed in powdered gold maki-e technique with accents of embedded ceramic fragments, lead inlay, and mother-of-pearl. The naturalistic rendering of the vine's tendrils and leaves demonstrates the technical virtuosity expected of Kyoto lacquer workshops.
assets.significance: The maker, Mochizuki Hanzan, was a specialist in combining maki-e with unusual inlay materials — ceramic, metal, and shell — creating surfaces of extraordinary textural complexity. This inrō entered Western collections through Howard Mansfield, a New York print connoisseur whose Japanese art acquisitions helped establish the Metropolitan Museum's holdings as among the finest outside Japan.

assets.title_en: Robe (Kosode) with Shells and Sea Grasses
assets.medium: Embroidery and gold leaf on plain-weave silk patterned with warp floats
assets.image_alt: Robe (Kosode) with Shells and Sea Grasses
assets.description: Scattered shells and undulating sea grasses spread across the deep indigo ground of this kosode, rendered through a combination of embroidery, resist-dyeing, and applied gold leaf. The textile dates to the early Edo period when kosode — literally 'small sleeves' — evolved from an undergarment into the outer robe that would eventually become the modern kimono. The marine motifs suggest connections to classical poetry traditions where shells symbolized fleeting beauty and the impermanence of the seaside.
assets.significance: Surviving early Edo kosode in this condition are exceptionally rare — most textiles from this era have been altered, remounted, or lost entirely. The interplay of embroidery, stencil-dyeing, and metallic foil on a single garment marks a transitional moment in Japanese textile art, when craftsmen first began treating the full surface of a robe as a unified pictorial canvas rather than a series of repeated patterns.

assets.medium: Iron, copper-gold alloy (shakudō), copper-silver alloy (shibuichi), gold, bronze, copper
assets.image_alt: Sword Guard (Tsuba)
assets.description: An iron tsuba (sword guard) from approximately 1800, decorated with multiple metalwork alloys including shakudō (copper-gold), shibuichi (copper-silver), gold, bronze, and copper. The combination of these distinct alloys — each with its characteristic color and patina — creates a painterly effect in metal, demonstrating the extraordinary sophistication of Edo-period sword furniture.
assets.significance: Tsuba represent the convergence of martial function and decorative art. By the late Edo period, with warfare centuries past, tsuba makers (tsubashi) had elevated the sword guard from practical hardware to miniature masterwork. Analysis of alloy composition and patination is central to authentication.

assets.medium: Iron, bronze, gold, silver, copper
assets.image_alt: Sword Guard (Tsuba) — Landscape Design
assets.description: An iron tsuba featuring a landscape design executed in multiple metals including bronze, gold, silver, and copper inlay. The miniature landscape — rendered on a guard measuring just 8.4 cm in height — demonstrates the Japanese metalworker's ability to create atmospheric depth and pictorial complexity within the constraints of a functional sword fitting.
assets.significance: Landscape tsuba are among the most prized categories of sword furniture, requiring the maker to translate the conventions of ink painting into the language of metal. The use of five distinct metals (iron, bronze, gold, silver, copper) in a single piece exemplifies the technical mastery of the Edo-period tsubashi tradition.

assets.title_en: Fudō Myōō, the Immovable Wisdom King (Achala Vidyaraja)
assets.medium: Japanese cypress with lacquer, color, gold, cut gold leaf (kirikane), and inlaid crystal eyes
assets.image_alt: Fudō Myōō, the Immovable Wisdom King
assets.description: Carved from Japanese cypress and finished with lacquer, polychromy, and inlaid crystal eyes, this Fudō Myōō radiates the fierce determination befitting the Immovable Wisdom King of Esoteric Buddhism. The figure's taut musculature, clenched jaw, and dynamic forward lean represent the Kamakura period's departure from the serene idealism of earlier Buddhist sculpture. The separately carved flame mandorla behind the figure intensifies the sense of spiritual energy, while the sword and binding rope in each hand symbolize the cutting of delusion and restraint of evil.
assets.significance: This work is attributed to the Kaikei workshop tradition and belongs to the revolutionary Kamakura-period approach to Buddhist sculpture: replacing idealized calm with visceral intensity. The use of gyokugan (crystal-inlaid eyes) — a technique perfected by the Kei school sculptors — creates a startlingly lifelike gaze that transforms a devotional object into something approaching portraiture.

assets.medium: Japanese cypress with lacquer and gold
assets.dimensions:
a: Diam. 21 in. (53.3 cm)
b: Diam. 21 in. (53.3 cm)
assets.image_alt: Flying Apsaras (Hiten)
assets.description: Two circular relief panels depict celestial beings in mid-flight, their robes streaming behind them as they descend through clouds — these hiten (flying apsaras) were originally architectural elements of a Buddhist temple interior. Carved from Japanese cypress during the late Heian period, each figure displays the soft, rounded facial features and flowing drapery that characterize the refined Fujiwara aesthetic. Traces of original polychromy survive in protected recesses, indicating that these panels once blazed with color and gilt.
assets.significance: Surviving Heian-period hiten of this quality are among the rarest examples of Japanese Buddhist architectural decoration extant. Temple interiors were once populated with such celestial figures — musicians, dancers, and devotees — creating an immersive vision of a Buddhist paradise. Most were lost to fires, earthquakes, and the Meiji-era temple consolidations, making each surviving example an irreplaceable document of medieval Japanese artistic and spiritual ambition.

assets.medium: Ivory with bronze disk inlaid with gold
assets.description: A kagamibuta-style netsuke — a shallow ivory bowl fitted with a bronze disk lid inlaid with gold. The kagamibuta ('mirror-lid') form is one of the most elegant netsuke types, combining the carver's skill in ivory with the metalworker's art of inlay. The gold design on the bronze disk creates a striking contrast against the warm patina of the ivory bowl.
assets.significance: Kagamibuta netsuke represent a collaboration between ivory carvers and metalworkers, making them particularly interesting from an authentication perspective — two distinct craft traditions must be evaluated in a single object.
assets.image_alt: Kagamibuta netsuke with gold inlay on bronze

assets.medium: Stoneware with ash glaze (Seto ware)
assets.description: A cluster of yama-chawan — 'mountain tea bowls' — fused together during firing. These utilitarian Seto ware bowls were mass-produced in medieval kilns and represent the everyday ceramic tradition that predated the tea ceremony's aesthetic revolution. Their accidental fusion during firing preserves a moment in the kiln's life, revealing stacking methods and firing temperatures.
assets.significance: Yama-chawan provide critical archaeological evidence for understanding medieval Japanese kiln technology. Their unpretentious simplicity contrasts sharply with the later tea ceremony wares, illustrating the vast aesthetic range of Japanese ceramics.
assets.image_alt: Cluster of mountain tea bowls (yama-chawan)

assets.medium: Album
assets.description: An album of paintings by Ike no Taiga (1723–1776), one of the foremost masters of the Nanga (literati painting) school. Taiga blended Chinese Southern School painting traditions with a distinctly Japanese lyricism, creating landscapes and figure studies of extraordinary freedom and spontaneity. The accompanying colophons by later scholars attest to the work's provenance and artistic significance.
assets.significance: Ike no Taiga is regarded as one of the twin pillars of Japanese literati painting alongside Yosa Buson. His works command exceptional prices at auction and are extensively documented in Japanese art scholarship. Albums like this provide rare insight into his working methods and range.
assets.image_alt: Paintings by Taigadō with Colophons

assets.title_en: Shirabyōshi Dancer and Female Servant; Courtesan and Girl Attendant
assets.title_jp: 白拍子と侍女、遊女と禿
assets.medium: Hanging scroll paintings, remounted as a two‑panel folding screen; ink, color, and gold on silk
assets.description: A pair of bijin-ga (beautiful women paintings) by Tsukioka Settei, depicting a shirabyōshi dancer with her servant and a courtesan with her young attendant (kamuro). The Osaka-based Settei was renowned for his elegant depictions of women, combining the decorative sensibility of the Kanō school with the intimacy of genre painting. The remounting as a folding screen suggests these works were treasured display pieces.
assets.significance: Tsukioka Settei was a pivotal figure in Osaka painting, bridging the Kanō academic tradition and the emerging ukiyo-e aesthetic. His grandson Tsukioka Yoshitoshi would become the last great master of ukiyo-e. These works exemplify the sophisticated culture of Osaka's merchant class.
assets.image_alt: Shirabyōshi Dancer and Female Servant; Courtesan and Girl Attendant

assets.title_en: Poem Accompanying an Over Robe (Uchikake) with a Bamboo Painting by Gion Nankai
assets.title_jp: 祇園南海筆竹画打掛に寄す詩
assets.medium: Handscroll; ink on silk
assets.description: A calligraphic handscroll by the celebrated Confucian scholar and historian Rai San'yō (1780–1832), composed as a poetic response to a painted over-robe decorated with bamboo by the earlier literati artist Gion Nankai. This work represents the literati ideal of interconnected arts — painting, poetry, and calligraphy forming a unified aesthetic experience.
assets.significance: Rai San'yō was among the most influential intellectuals of late Edo Japan. His historical writings helped shape the ideology that would drive the Meiji Restoration. This scroll demonstrates the interpenetration of visual art and literary culture that defines the bunjin (literati) tradition.
assets.image_alt: Poem Accompanying an Over Robe (Uchikake) with a Bamboo Painting by Gion Nankai

assets.title_en: Blade and Mounting for a Dagger (Tantō)
assets.title_jp: 短刀 銘 月山貞一
assets.medium: Steel, wood (rosewod), copper-gold alloy (shakudō), copper-silver alloy (shibuichi), gold, silver
assets.description: A tantō (dagger) by Gassan Sadakazu (1836–1918), the founder of the modern Gassan lineage. Sadakazu revived the ancient ayasugi-hada (wave-pattern grain) forging technique originally developed by the medieval Gassan smiths of Dewa province. The blade dates to 1870, just before the Meiji government's sword-abolishment edict of 1876. The luxurious mounting in rosewood with shakudō and gold fittings reflects the highest level of Meiji-era craftsmanship.
assets.significance: Gassan Sadakazu was designated a Teishitsu Gigeiin (Imperial Household Artist) — the Meiji-era equivalent of a Living National Treasure. His revival of the ayasugi-hada technique saved a medieval forging tradition from extinction. This tantō represents the transition from shinshintō to gendaitō production.
assets.image_alt: Blade and Mounting for a Dagger (Tantō) by Gassan Sadakazu
assets.dimensions: L. 16 1/8 in. (40.9 cm); L. of blade 14 7/16 in. (36.7 cm); L. of cutting edge 10 1/2 in. (26.7 cm); L. of knife (kozuka) 7 1/4 in. (18.4 cm)

assets.medium: Pair of hanging scrolls; ink on paper
assets.description: A pair of hanging scrolls by Kanō Naizen, depicting Chinese literary figures in bold ink brushwork characteristic of the Kanō school. The compositions demonstrate the school's mastery of Chinese subject matter reinterpreted through Japanese sensibility.
assets.significance: Kanō Naizen was a prominent painter of the Momoyama era, known for his dynamic compositions and confident brushwork. These scrolls exemplify the Kanō school's role as the dominant force in Japanese official painting.
assets.image_alt: Zheng Huangniu and Yushanzhu by Kanō Naizen

assets.title_en: Noh Robe (Karaori) with Pattern of Bamboo and Young Pines on Bands of Red and White — Bamboo and Young Pines
assets.title_jp: 能装束 唐織 竹若松文
assets.medium: Twill-weave silk with resist-dyed warps, supplementary weft patterning in silk, and gold-leaf paper strips (karaori)
assets.description: A karaori — the most luxurious type of Noh costume, reserved for female roles. The name literally means 'Chinese weave,' reflecting the technique's continental origins. This example features alternating bands of red and white ground with patterns of bamboo and young pine, symbols of resilience and new beginnings. The gold-leaf paper strips woven into the fabric create a shimmering effect under stage lighting.
assets.significance: Karaori are among the most technically demanding textiles ever produced. Each costume represents months of specialized weaving, with some examples containing over twenty colors of silk thread supplemented by gold and silver leaf. These garments are themselves works of art, often valued independently of the performances they adorned.
assets.image_alt: Noh Robe (Karaori) with bamboo and young pines pattern

assets.title_en: Noh Costume (Nuihaku) with Books and Nandina Branches — Books and Nandina Branches
assets.title_jp: 能装束 縫箔 書物南天文
assets.medium: Silk embroidery and metallic leaf on silk satin
assets.description: A nuihaku costume combining two of the most refined textile techniques: nui (embroidery) and haku (metallic leaf application). The design of scattered books and nandina branches carries layered literary allusions — books symbolize learning and cultivation, while nandina (nanten) is a wordplay on 'overcoming hardship' (nan wo tenzuru). The silk satin ground provides a luminous backdrop for the intricate metallic patterns.
assets.significance: Nuihaku costumes represent the intersection of painting, embroidery, and metalwork on fabric. The combination of silk thread embroidery with gold and silver leaf creates textural contrasts that would be impossible in any single medium. These costumes are among the most prized objects in Japanese textile art.
assets.image_alt: Noh Costume (Nuihaku) with books and nandina branches

assets.medium: Resist-dyed and painted (bingata) silk crepe
assets.description: A vibrant bingata robe from the Ryūkyū Kingdom — the brilliant textile tradition of Okinawa that synthesized influences from China, Southeast Asia, and mainland Japan into something utterly original. Bingata employs stencil-resist dyeing with hand-painted details, producing the vivid, saturated colors and bold patterns that distinguish this tradition from all others in East Asia.
assets.significance: Bingata textiles represent one of the most distinctive regional textile traditions in Japan. The Ryūkyū Kingdom's position as a maritime trading hub created a unique aesthetic that blended tropical exuberance with refined technique. Today, bingata is recognized as an Important Intangible Cultural Property of Japan.
assets.image_alt: Ryūkyūan bingata robe with vibrant resist-dyed patterns

assets.medium: Woodblock print (tan-e); ink and color on paper
assets.description: An early ukiyo-e print by Torii Kiyomasu I, one of the founders of the Torii school that dominated kabuki theater poster design. This tan-e (orange-printed picture) depicts a courtesan of the Myōgaya house in the Yoshiwara pleasure quarter. The bold, calligraphic outlines and hand-applied orange pigment are characteristic of pre-nishiki-e printing technology.
assets.significance: Tan-e represent a critical early stage in ukiyo-e development, before the invention of multi-color printing (nishiki-e) in the 1760s. The Torii school's dynamic, poster-like compositions established the visual vocabulary of kabuki-related prints that would flourish for the next century.
assets.image_alt: Courtesan from the Myōgaya House by Torii Kiyomasu I

assets.medium: Woodblock print; ink and color on paper
assets.description: A genre scene by Kitagawa Utamaro showing women attending a theatrical performance. Utamaro's genius lay in capturing the subtle psychology of his subjects — here, the varying degrees of attention, distraction, and social interaction among the viewers create a nuanced social portrait. The print demonstrates his mastery of the nishiki-e full-color technique.
assets.significance: Utamaro transformed the bijin-ga genre from idealized portraiture to genuine psychological study. His works were among the first ukiyo-e to reach Europe, profoundly influencing Impressionist and Art Nouveau artists. This print dates to his early mature period, before the iconic ōkubi-e (large-head portraits) that would make him famous.
assets.image_alt: Seeing a Performance by Kitagawa Utamaro

assets.medium: Triptych of woodblock prints; ink and color on paper
assets.description: A triptych by Utamaro illustrating the famous episode from the Tales of Ise (Ise Monogatari) in which the Heian-period poet Ariwara no Narihira journeys eastward from the capital. Utamaro transposes the classical literary scene into the Edo-period present, dressing the travelers in contemporary clothing — a characteristic ukiyo-e device called mitate-e (parody picture) that creates layered meanings for educated viewers.
assets.significance: Mitate-e — the playful re-staging of classical literary and historical scenes in contemporary settings — is one of ukiyo-e's most sophisticated devices. This triptych demonstrates Utamaro's range beyond the bijin-ga genre, engaging with Japan's deepest literary traditions while maintaining the visual appeal of his contemporary subjects.
assets.image_alt: Narihira's Journey to the East by Kitagawa Utamaro

assets.title_en: Netsuke of Snail on a Box
assets.medium: Wood
assets.description: A carved wood netsuke depicting a snail perched atop a small box — a subject that embodies the netsuke carver's delight in transforming everyday observations into miniature art. The spiral shell is rendered with naturalistic precision, while the box beneath provides a stable, compact form ideal for the toggle's practical function. Signed by Shigemasa.
assets.significance: Animal subjects were among the most popular themes in netsuke carving, and snails — with their perfect spiral geometry — presented an irresistible challenge to the miniaturist. The inclusion of the box creates a visual pun on containment and concealment.
assets.image_alt: Netsuke of snail on a box by Shigemasa

assets.title_en: Netsuke of Seated Shishi Clutching a Ball
assets.medium: Ebony with gold and horn inlay
assets.description: A seated shishi (lion-dog) clutching a brocade ball, carved in dense ebony and enriched with gold and horn inlay. The guardian beast's mane is rendered in tight, rhythmic curls, while its muscular body coils with contained energy. The inlaid eyes give the creature an alert, almost sentient presence.
assets.significance: Shishi are among the most symbolically charged subjects in netsuke carving — guardians against evil, they appear throughout Japanese art as protectors of sacred spaces. The use of ebony with precious inlays indicates this was made for a discriminating collector.
assets.image_alt: Netsuke of seated shishi with ball by Ryōzan

assets.medium: Black lacquer and nashiji ground with gold and silver iroe togidashi and hiramaki-e Netsuke: Kintarō with forest animals on peach stone; ivory, mother-of-pearl, tortoise shell Ojime: melon; red bronze with iron and gilding
assets.description: An exquisite inrō by Koma Yasutada, one of the most celebrated lacquer artists of the Edo period. Peacocks and flowers are rendered in multiple maki-e techniques — togidashi (polished-out), hiramaki-e (flat), and iroe (colored) — creating extraordinary depth and luminosity against a nashiji ground that glows like scattered gold dust. The accompanying netsuke of Kintarō carved on a peach stone demonstrates equally astonishing miniature craftsmanship.
assets.significance: The Koma family was one of the great dynasties of Edo-period lacquer art, serving the Tokugawa shōguns. Yasutada's works are prized for their technical virtuosity and refined composition, combining multiple maki-e techniques in a single piece.
assets.image_alt: Inrō with peacocks and flowers by Koma Yasutada

assets.title_jp: 印籠 扇面散らし文
assets.medium: Lacquered wood with gold and silver togidashimaki-e and hiramaki-e Ojime: lacquer bead with maki-e decoration Netsuke: ivory toggle of Shōki, the Demon Queller
assets.description: Scattered fans — some open, some folded — drift across the surface of this inrō like leaves on water, rendered in togidashimaki-e (polished-out sprinkled design) that creates an almost photographic smoothness. The fan motif is quintessentially Japanese: an everyday object elevated to a symbol of elegance, transience, and the aesthetic pleasure of arrangement.
assets.significance: The 'scattered fan' (senmen-chirashi) is one of the most beloved decorative motifs in Japanese art, appearing across lacquerware, ceramics, textiles, and painting. On this inrō, the motif serves both as decoration and as a meditation on the art of composition itself.
assets.image_alt: Inrō with floating fans design

assets.medium: Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink and color on gold leaf on paper
assets.dimensions:
Image (each screen): 64 7/16 in. x 11ft. 6 3/4 in. (163.7 x 352.4 cm)
Overall (each screen): 70 1/2 in. x 12 ft. 2 1/4 in. (179.1 x 371.5 cm)
assets.description: Ogata Kōrin's monumental interpretation of irises at Yatsuhashi — the famous eight-plank bridge from the Tales of Ise — is one of the supreme achievements of Japanese decorative art. The bold blue irises and green leaves are painted directly on a brilliant gold-leaf ground with no preliminary drawing, each brushstroke placed with absolute conviction. The bridge itself is implied but invisible, leaving the viewer to navigate the composition as a traveler would cross the marshland.
assets.significance: This pair of screens is among the most important works in the Metropolitan Museum's Asian art collection. Kōrin's radical simplification — reducing a narrative landscape to just irises and gold — anticipated abstract art by two centuries. The screens exemplify the byōbu tradition where folding screens function as both architectural element and painting.
assets.image_alt: Irises at Yatsuhashi by Ogata Kōrin, folding screens

assets.medium: Pair of six-panel screens; ink, color, and gold leaf on paper
assets.description: A pair of monumental screens by Tosa Mitsunobu depicting bamboo across the four seasons — from spring shoots to winter's snow-laden stalks. The Tosa school specialized in yamato-e (native Japanese painting), and these screens demonstrate their mastery of naturalistic observation combined with decorative gold-leaf techniques. Each panel captures a different moment in bamboo's annual cycle, creating a continuous panorama of time passing.
assets.significance: Tosa Mitsunobu was the leading painter of the yamato-e tradition in the Muromachi period. These screens represent the intersection of painting and architecture — designed to define and transform interior space, they function as both art objects and architectural elements in the shoin-zukuri room.
assets.image_alt: Bamboo in the Four Seasons screens by Tosa Mitsunobu

assets.title_en: Orchid Pavilion Gathering; Autumn Harvest Festival
assets.title_jp: 蘭亭曲水宴・月次風俗図屏風
assets.medium: Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink and color on paper
assets.dimensions:
Image (each): 63 1/4 in. × 11 ft. 8 3/16 in. (160.7 × 356 cm)
Overall with mounting:  69 9/16 in. × 12 ft. 2 7/16 in. (176.7 × 372 cm)
assets.description: Ike no Taiga — one of the towering figures of Japanese literati (bunjin-ga) painting — transforms two classical subjects into immersive architectural environments. The Orchid Pavilion scene depicts the legendary gathering of Chinese scholars, while the Autumn Harvest Festival captures Japanese seasonal celebration. Both screens demonstrate Taiga's distinctive fusion of Chinese and Japanese painting traditions.
assets.significance: Folding screens (byōbu) literally mean 'wind walls' — they are architecture as much as art. Taiga's screens exemplify the Japanese principle that interior space should be composed, not merely decorated. The screens would have been displayed seasonally in a formal reception room, transforming the character of the space itself.
assets.image_alt: Orchid Pavilion Gathering screens by Ike no Taiga

assets.title_en: Potted Pine Tree Drum and Seven Herbs Planted in a Box
assets.title_jp: 鉢植松・太鼓・箱入り七草
assets.medium: Woodblock print (surimono); ink and color on paper
assets.description: A surimono by Sunayama Gosei depicting a potted pine tree alongside a drum and a box planted with the seven herbs of spring (nanakusa). Surimono were privately commissioned prints of exceptional quality, produced in limited editions for poetry circles and special occasions. The potted pine (hachi-ue no matsu) is a New Year's motif symbolizing longevity and constancy — its careful training in a container reflects the bonsai aesthetic of nature refined through human patience.
assets.significance: This print captures the deep cultural connection between potted trees and Japanese seasonal celebration. The potted pine is both a decorative element and a philosophical statement — nature contained, shaped, and revered. The surimono format, with its superior paper and metallic pigments, elevates a seasonal greeting into fine art.
assets.image_alt: Potted Pine Tree, Drum and Seven Herbs Planted in a Box by Sunayama Gosei, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: Kakemono of Monkey, Wine Cup and Potted Plants
assets.title_jp: 掛物 猿・盃・鉢植え
assets.medium: Woodblock print (surimono); ink and color on paper
assets.description: A painted monkey observes a wine cup and potted plants in this surimono — a privately commissioned print of exceptional refinement, distributed among poetry circles rather than sold commercially. The composition combines Keisai Eisen's skill as a painter-designer with the printer's mastery of metallic pigments, blind embossing, and gradated color that elevate surimono above ordinary woodblock prints. The monkey-and-wine-cup motif carries zodiacal and seasonal associations tied to the occasion for which the print was commissioned.
assets.significance: Eisen (1790–1848) is primarily remembered for bijin-ga (portraits of beautiful women), but his surimono reveal a more intimate, intellectually playful side of his art. Surimono functioned as luxury calling cards within Edo literary salons — each one a collaboration between poet, artist, and printer, produced in small editions for private exchange. The survival rate is low, making fine examples keenly sought by collectors.
assets.image_alt: Kakemono of Monkey, Wine Cup and Potted Plants by Keisai Eisen, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.medium: Four sliding-door panels (fusuma); ink, color, gold, and gold leaf on paper
assets.dimensions:
Overall (of all four panels): 68 3/4 x 191 1/8 in. (174.6 x 485.5 cm)
Overall (a): 68 3/8 x 47 5/8 in. (173.7 x 121 cm)
Overall (b): 68 3/8 x 48 3/4 in. (173.7 x 123.8 cm)
Overall (c): 68 1/2 x 47 3/4 in. (174 x 121.3 cm)
Overall (d): 68 1/2 x 47 3/4 in. (174 x 121.3 cm)
assets.description: One of the most celebrated works in Japanese painting — Kano Sansetsu's monumental 'Old Plum' originally decorated the fusuma (sliding doors) of Tenshōin, a sub-temple of Myōshin-ji in Kyoto. The ancient plum tree erupts from the lower left in a massive, gnarled trunk that twists and branches across all four panels with explosive energy. Young blossoms burst from aged wood — a profound metaphor for renewal emerging from endurance. The gold-leaf ground transforms the wall surface into an infinite, radiant space.
assets.significance: Kano Sansetsu (1589–1651) inherited the Kyoto branch of the Kanō school and pushed its decorative vocabulary to surreal extremes. This work's dramatic tension between the organic, almost violent trunk and the delicate blossoms creates an effect that feels startlingly modern — a quality that has made it one of the Metropolitan Museum's most iconic Asian art holdings.
assets.image_alt: Old Plum by Kano Sansetsu, 1646, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.title_en: Goose and Reeds; Willows in the Moonlight
assets.medium: Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink, color and gold on paper
assets.description: A pair of six-panel screens by Maruyama Ōkyo (1733–1795), the revolutionary painter who introduced Western perspective and direct observation from nature into Japanese painting. The right screen depicts a goose descending through reeds with startling naturalistic precision; the left screen captures willows bathed in moonlight with atmospheric subtlety. Created nearly twenty years apart (1774 and 1793), together they span Ōkyo's mature career and demonstrate his evolving mastery of light, atmosphere, and natural form.
assets.significance: Maruyama Ōkyo founded the Maruyama school, which championed shasei (painting from life) over copying classical models — a philosophical break as significant in Japanese art as Impressionism was in Europe. These screens, from the Mary Griggs Burke Collection, exemplify the union of decorative grandeur with observational truth that made Ōkyo's work revolutionary. His influence extends directly to modern Japanese painting (nihon-ga).
assets.image_alt: Goose and Reeds; Willows in the Moonlight by Maruyama Ōkyo, The Metropolitan Museum of Art

assets.medium: Wood core, dry lacquer, traces of gold leaf
assets.description: A seated bodhisattva from the Nara period, constructed using the dry lacquer (kanshitsu) technique — layers of hemp cloth soaked in lacquer over a wood core. This technically demanding method, imported from Tang-dynasty China, produced sculptures of remarkable lightness and refined detail. The traces of gold leaf on the surface indicate this figure once gleamed with sacred radiance in a temple interior. The serene expression and balanced proportions exemplify the cosmopolitan Buddhist art that flourished under Emperor Shōmu's patronage.
assets.significance: Nara-period dry lacquer sculptures are among the rarest surviving examples of early Japanese Buddhist art. The kanshitsu technique was largely abandoned after the Nara period in favor of wood carving, making extant examples exceptionally valuable for understanding the technical transmission from continental Asia.
assets.image_alt: Seated Bodhisattva, 8th century Nara period, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Wood with traces of polychromy
assets.description: A Kamakura-period guardian figure (Shukongōjin/Vajrapāṇi) carved in wood with vestiges of original polychrome decoration. The dynamic pose — weight shifted forward, arm raised as if wielding a vajra thunderbolt — exemplifies the Kei school's revolutionary approach to Buddhist sculpture. The naturalistic musculature and fierce expression reflect the period's emphasis on visceral spiritual power, a departure from the serene idealism of earlier Heian-period figures.
assets.significance: Kamakura-period guardian sculptures represent the zenith of Japanese wood carving. The Kei school, led by Unkei and his sons, introduced an unprecedented realism that would define Japanese sculptural aesthetics for centuries. Crystal inlaid eyes (gyokugan) and dynamic compositions were their hallmarks.
assets.image_alt: Shukongōjin (Vajrapāṇi), 13th century Kamakura period, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.title_en: Fudō Myō-ō — The Immovable Wisdom King
assets.medium: Wood with polychromy and gilt-bronze accessories
assets.description: Fudō Myō-ō (Acala), the most important of the Five Wisdom Kings in Esoteric Buddhism, rendered in polychromed wood with gilt-bronze sword and noose accessories. His wrathful expression — bared fangs, bulging eyes, furrowed brow — is precisely codified in iconographic texts: the left eye looks downward, the right upward, and one fang points up while the other points down. He sits upon a rock pedestal (banjakuza), surrounded by flames (kōhaien) that consume worldly delusion.
assets.significance: Fudō Myō-ō images are the single most common subject in Japanese Esoteric Buddhist sculpture. This Kamakura-period example preserves its original polychrome and metalwork accessories — a rare level of completeness that makes it invaluable for understanding how these figures appeared in their original ritual context.
assets.image_alt: Fudō Myō-ō (Acala), 13th century Kamakura period, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Steel, wood, lacquer, ray skin (karagi), gold, and copper-gold alloy (shakudo)
assets.description: A tantō (dagger) by Fujiwara Teruhiro in elaborate Edo-period mountings. The handle is wrapped in ray skin (samegawa) — the shagreen grip that provides both aesthetic texture and functional purchase. The fittings feature shakudō (copper-gold alloy) and gold, demonstrating the sophisticated metalworking that elevated sword furniture to independent art form. The lacquer scabbard (saya) adds another layer of craft mastery to this composite work.
assets.significance: Edo-period tantō mountings represent the convergence of multiple Japanese craft traditions — swordsmithing, metalwork, lacquer, and textile wrapping — in a single object. Authentication requires expertise in each of these distinct disciplines, making them particularly challenging and rewarding objects for connoisseurship.
assets.image_alt: Dagger (Tanto), Edo period, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Color woodblock print; vertical nagaban
assets.description: A vertical nagaban print by Katsushika Hokusai depicting cranes perched on the snow-laden branches of an ancient pine. The pine tree (matsu) — symbol of longevity, constancy, and endurance — is the single most important tree in Japanese aesthetic culture, from bonsai cultivation to garden design to poetic metaphor. Hokusai captures the tree's dual nature: gnarled, powerful branches bearing the weight of snow while cranes — themselves symbols of immortality — rest in serene composure.
assets.significance: Hokusai's nature prints demonstrate the deep observational tradition that connects Japanese painting to the bonsai aesthetic. The trained eye that appreciates a centuries-old pine bonsai — reading character in bark texture, balance in branch placement, narrative in trunk movement — is the same eye that reads Hokusai's compositions.
assets.image_alt: Cranes on Snow-covered Pine by Katsushika Hokusai, c. 1834, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Hanging scroll; ink and colors on silk
assets.description: A bijin-ga (beautiful woman painting) by Miyagawa Chōshun, depicting an elegant woman partially concealed behind a folding screen. Chōshun was unique among Edo-period artists — he worked exclusively in painting, never producing woodblock prints, which made his original works exceptionally rare even in his own time. The interplay between the visible and concealed, the painted figure and the painted screen-within-the-painting, creates a sophisticated meditation on representation and desire.
assets.significance: Miyagawa Chōshun (1683–1753) founded the Miyagawa school, one of the few ukiyo-e lineages that remained exclusively painters rather than print designers. His works represent the 'high art' end of floating-world imagery — created for wealthy patrons rather than mass audiences. This artistic tension between elite and popular culture is precisely the dynamic that contemporary Japanese artists like Murakami revisit in their Superflat theory.
assets.image_alt: A Beauty Behind a Screen by Miyagawa Chōshun, c. 1750, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Lacquer
assets.description: A multi-compartment inrō decorated with a mountainous landscape in gold maki-e (sprinkled picture) on black lacquer ground. Inrō were portable medicine containers worn at the waist, suspended by a silk cord from the obi sash and secured with a netsuke toggle above. Though functionally utilitarian, inrō became canvases for the highest achievements of Japanese lacquer art — their small scale demanded extraordinary precision and mastery of the maki-e technique.
assets.significance: The inrō represents the fusion of craft and fashion in Edo-period Japan. As accessories of the samurai and merchant classes, they evolved from practical containers into competitive displays of aesthetic taste and patronage. The finest examples rival any form of miniature art in technical sophistication.
assets.image_alt: Inrō Case with landscape design, Edo period, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Lacquer with inlay
assets.description: An inrō by Somada Mitsumine featuring the distinctive Somada technique — thin slivers of iridescent abalone shell (aogai) inlaid into lacquer to create shimmering decorative patterns. The Somada school, based in Nagasaki, developed this technique to a level of refinement unmatched elsewhere. Each fragment of shell is individually cut, shaped, and positioned to exploit its natural iridescence, creating an effect that shifts with viewing angle and light.
assets.significance: Somada lacquerware represents a rare intersection of Japanese lacquer tradition with the cosmopolitan culture of Nagasaki, Japan's sole window to the outside world during the isolation period. The Somada technique is prized by collectors for its optical brilliance and the extreme difficulty of its execution.
assets.image_alt: Inrō Case with Somada inlay by Somada Mitsumine, early 19th century, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Carved wood
assets.description: A wood netsuke from the Edo period, carved with the functional precision and artistic expression that define this uniquely Japanese art form. Netsuke served as toggles to secure inrō, tobacco pouches, and other hanging objects (sagemono) to the waistband of a kimono. Because the kimono lacks pockets, these small sculptures were born from practical necessity and evolved into one of the most distinctive genres of Japanese miniature art.
assets.significance: Wood netsuke represent the earliest and most traditional form of the art. Unlike ivory or staghorn, wood allowed carvers to exploit grain patterns and color variations as integral elements of the design. The finest wood netsuke demonstrate the Japanese aesthetic of working with natural materials rather than against them.
assets.image_alt: Netsuke, carved wood figure, Edo period, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Ivory
assets.description: An ivory netsuke from the 18th century, exemplifying the mastery of miniature carving that made Japanese netsuke internationally celebrated from the Meiji era onward. Ivory provided a smooth, dense medium that allowed the most intricate detail — facial expressions, textile textures, animal fur — to be rendered at a scale of just a few centimeters. The warm patina that develops over decades of handling by its owner gives each piece a unique history written on its surface.
assets.significance: Ivory netsuke were among the first Japanese art objects to capture European and American collectors in the late 19th century, catalyzed by Japan's display at the 1867 Paris Exposition. The V&A holds one of the world's great netsuke collections, numbering over 400 pieces and providing essential reference for authentication and connoisseurship.
assets.image_alt: Netsuke, ivory miniature, 18th century, Victoria and Albert Museum

assets.medium: Six-panel screen (one of pair); Ink, colors, gold leaf, and gold powder on silk
assets.description: A magnificent six-panel folding screen by Tosa Mitsuoki depicting autumn maple branches laden with tanzaku — slips of paper inscribed with classical poems. The composition cascades diagonally across all six panels against a sumptuous gold-leaf ground, creating an immersive environment of seasonal poetry. Folding screens (byōbu) were the primary means of defining and decorating interior space in traditional Japanese architecture — they functioned simultaneously as room dividers, backdrops for ceremonial occasions, and monumental paintings.
assets.significance: Tosa Mitsuoki (1617–1691) revived the Tosa school of yamato-e painting, reasserting its courtly elegance against the dominant Kanō school. This screen exemplifies the integration of painting, poetry, and architecture that defines the Japanese interior — where every surface is a potential canvas and every division of space is an artistic decision.
assets.image_alt: Autumn Maples with Poem Slips by Tosa Mitsuoki, c. 1675, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Six-panel screen; ink, color, and gold on paper
assets.description: A six-panel screen depicting maize plants and cockscombs with botanical precision against a gold-leaf ground. The subject is notable — maize was introduced to Japan from the Americas via Portuguese traders in the 16th century, and its appearance in formal screen painting reflects the cultural impact of the Nanban (Southern Barbarian) trade. The artist renders the exotic plants with the same reverent attention given to traditional subjects like pine and plum, integrating the foreign into the Japanese aesthetic vocabulary.
assets.significance: This screen embodies the adaptability of Japanese decorative art — absorbing foreign influences while maintaining the formal conventions of the gold-ground screen tradition. As architectural furnishing, such screens transformed domestic interiors into curated environments where every visual element carried cultural meaning.
assets.image_alt: Maize and Cockscombs, mid 17th century six-panel screen, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.title_en: Tsuba — Iron Sword Guard with Inlay
assets.medium: Iron with gold and silver inlay
assets.description: An iron tsuba (sword guard) with gold and silver inlay from the late Edo period. The tsuba protected the swordsman's hand and provided a counterbalance to the blade, but its aesthetic function was equally important — it was the most visible decorative element of a mounted sword and served as a direct expression of the owner's taste and status. Edo-period tsuba makers (tsubashi) developed specialized schools, each with distinct stylistic signatures in metalwork technique, motif selection, and surface treatment.
assets.significance: Tsuba collecting is one of the oldest and most developed areas of Japanese art connoisseurship. The identification of school, period, and maker through analysis of iron quality, carving style, and inlay technique constitutes a sophisticated forensic discipline with centuries of accumulated scholarship. The V&A's tsuba collection offers essential comparative material for authentication.
assets.image_alt: Tsuba, iron sword guard with gold and silver inlay, c. 1800-1850, Victoria and Albert Museum

assets.title_en: Pair of Menuki — Sword Grip Ornaments
assets.medium: Gold, shakudō (copper-gold alloy)
assets.description: A pair of menuki — small ornamental fittings placed beneath the wrapping of a katana's handle to improve grip and add decorative refinement. Though only a few centimeters in size, menuki demonstrate some of the most intricate metalwork in Japanese art. Carved in high relief from shakudō (a copper-gold alloy that develops a rich blue-black patina) and detailed with gold highlights, each pair tells a miniature narrative drawn from literature, nature, or martial legend.
assets.significance: Menuki represent the extreme miniaturization of Japanese metalwork craft. Their authentication requires close examination of alloy composition, patination, carving technique, and iconographic convention — skills that overlap significantly with netsuke and inrō connoisseurship. Together with tsuba, kozuka, and fuchi-kashira, they form the complete vocabulary of sword furniture (tōsōgu) collecting.
assets.image_alt: Pair of Menuki, gold and shakudō sword grip ornaments, c. 1660-1724, Victoria and Albert Museum

assets.medium: Color woodblock print; surimono
assets.description: A surimono by Niwa Tokei depicting an aged pine tree paired with camellias — a classic combination in Japanese garden design symbolizing the constancy of the pine through winter (endurance) and the camellia's promise of renewal (vitality). The surimono format, with its premium paper and metallic pigments, transforms this seasonal pairing into a jewel-like object. The pine's gnarled trunk and carefully shaped branches echo the aesthetic principles of bonsai — nature refined through human attention into an expression of time itself.
assets.significance: The pine-and-camellia pairing (matsu-tsubaki) is one of the canonical compositions in Japanese arts, appearing across painting, garden design, and bonsai arrangement. Understanding these symbolic associations is essential for authenticating and evaluating the cultural context of Japanese art objects across multiple categories.
assets.image_alt: A Pine Tree and Camellias by Niwa Tokei, 1840, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Color woodblock print; otanzaku
assets.description: An otanzaku-format print by Utagawa Hiroshige depicting a macaw perched on a pine branch. The exotic bird — a luxury import from Southeast Asia — contrasts dramatically with the ancient, weathered pine, creating a dialogue between the foreign and the indigenous, the vivid and the venerable. Hiroshige's nature prints are masterclasses in compositional economy: a single branch, a single bird, yet the entire relationship between nature, culture, and beauty is articulated.
assets.significance: Hiroshige's kachō-e (bird-and-flower prints) are the most collected genre of Japanese prints after his landscape series. Their appeal lies in the same sensibility that animates bonsai culture — the distillation of natural beauty into its essential elements, framed and elevated by human aesthetic judgment.
assets.image_alt: Macaw on a Pine Branch by Utagawa Hiroshige, c. 1835, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.title_en: Inrō Decorated with the Animals of the Zodiac
assets.medium: Lacquer
assets.description: An inrō by Koma Kyūhaku decorated with the twelve animals of the Chinese zodiac in gold maki-e on a rich black lacquer ground. The Koma school of lacquerers served the Tokugawa shōgunate for generations, perfecting the technique of togidashi maki-e — where the sprinkled gold design is buried under layers of lacquer and then polished flush with the surface, creating an effect of images emerging from the material itself. This conceptual integration of surface and image is a direct precursor to the 'Superflat' aesthetic theorized by Murakami.
assets.significance: The Koma school represents the pinnacle of official Edo-period lacquer art. Koma Kyūhaku was one of several generations bearing this name, each serving as maki-e shi (lacquer artist) to the shōgunate. Their work bridges the traditional craft hierarchy and contemporary conceptual frameworks — making it ideal for the 'contemporary' context of re-examining historical mastery through modern critical lenses.
assets.image_alt: Inrō with Animals of the Zodiac by Koma Kyūhaku, 19th century, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.title_en: Lovers, a Cat, and a Mouse in front of a Folding Screen
assets.medium: Woodblock print; oban, sumizuri-e
assets.description: An early ukiyo-e print attributed to Sugimura Jihei, one of the pioneers of the floating-world genre. The composition — lovers in an intimate domestic scene, a cat stalking a mouse, a painted folding screen as backdrop — layers multiple levels of watching and being watched. The folding screen depicted within the print creates a mise en abyme (image within an image) that anticipates post-modern reflexivity by three centuries. This play between reality and representation is fundamental to understanding the conceptual roots of contemporary Japanese visual culture.
assets.significance: Sugimura Jihei (active c. 1681–1703) worked in the earliest phase of ukiyo-e, when the genre was developing its characteristic subjects and formats. This print demonstrates that the self-referential, conceptually layered visual strategies associated with 'contemporary' art have deep roots in Japanese visual tradition — a key argument for understanding why Japan has been uniquely productive in generating art that bridges traditional and avant-garde categories.
assets.image_alt: Lovers, a Cat, and a Mouse by Sugimura Jihei, c. 1684/98, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Glazed stoneware
assets.description: An Oribe-type ewer (mizusashi or water vessel) with the boldly asymmetric form and distinctive green copper glaze that defines this quintessentially Japanese ceramic style. Named after tea master Furuta Oribe (1544–1615), Oribe ware deliberately rejected the symmetry and restraint of Chinese ceramic ideals in favor of distortion, spontaneity, and visual drama. The partial application of green glaze over iron-painted geometric motifs — leaving portions of the clay body exposed — embodies the wabi-sabi aesthetic of finding beauty in imperfection.
assets.significance: Oribe ware represents a revolutionary moment in Japanese ceramics: the deliberate assertion of a distinctly Japanese aesthetic identity independent of Chinese models. Furuta Oribe's tea ceremony philosophy demanded vessels that provoked surprise and delight through unconventional forms. Authentication of early Oribe requires analysis of clay body (Mino region clays have distinctive characteristics), glaze chemistry (the copper green is kiln-atmosphere dependent), and the degree of intentional distortion — later copies tend to exaggerate asymmetry beyond period norms.
assets.image_alt: Oribe-Type Ewer, early 17th century, Mino Province, Art Institute of Chicago

assets.medium: Silk and gold-leaf-over-lacquered-paper strips, warp-float faced 2:1 'Z' twill weave with supplementary patterning wefts; lined with silk, plain weave; dyed with safflower, beni
assets.description: An atsuita karaori (thick-woven Chinese-style weave) Noh costume of extraordinary complexity. The fabric combines silk threads with kinran — strips of gold leaf applied over lacquered paper, then cut into narrow ribbons and woven into the textile. The resulting surface shimmers with dimensional gold against the silk ground, creating the visual weight and presence essential for Noh theater, where costumes must read from the back of a large stage under flickering torchlight. The pattern of interlocking botanical motifs follows codified conventions that indicate the character type (usually a noblewoman or celestial being) the robe was designed to portray.
assets.significance: Noh costumes represent the summit of Japanese textile art. The atsuita weave is among the most technically demanding textile structures ever devised — requiring coordination of multiple warp and weft systems on specialized looms. Nishijin (Kyoto) weavers who produce these textiles are the inheritors of a tradition that has supplied the imperial court and major Noh schools for centuries. Authentication involves analysis of weave structure, gold-leaf application method, and comparison with documented costumes in the collections of major Noh families.
assets.image_alt: Atsuita Karaori Noh Costume, late Edo period, Art Institute of Chicago
